expectations.text: The Aha Moment correctly captures that chunk boundaries (not model size) were the root cause of retrieval failures
expectations.evidence: The Aha Moment reads: 'If your chunks are cutting across paragraph boundaries, you're feeding the embedding model broken context — and no embedding model, no matter how good, can recover meaning from a fragment that lost its beginning or end.' This explicitly identifies chunk boundaries as root cause and explicitly rules out model size as the lever.

expectations.text: The first 1-2 lines of each post create genuine curiosity through a surprising or counterintuitive claim
expectations.evidence: Variation 1 opens with: 'I spent time swapping embedding models in my RAG pipeline. / MiniLM to a larger model. Barely moved my RAGAS scores.' — counterintuitive and immediately engaging. Variation 3 opens with: 'Chunking strategy moved my RAG quality by 28%. Embedding model swap barely moved it at all.' — clear counterintuitive claim from line 1. However, Variation 2 opens with: 'I ran two sets of experiments on my RAG pipeline. / First: swap the embedding model.' — neutral scene-setting with no surprising claim. The curiosity hook in V2 does not land until several paragraphs in. The expectation requires genuine curiosity in the first 1-2 lines for each post; V2 does not satisfy this.

expectations.text: The posts include concrete details (28%, RAGAS, MiniLM) rather than generic motivational language
expectations.evidence: All three variations explicitly reference '28%', 'RAGAS', and 'MiniLM'. Variation 1: 'RAGAS jumped 28%', 'MiniLM to a larger model'. Variation 2: 'RAGAS jumped 28%', 'MiniLM can produce a signal'. Variation 3: 'moved my RAG quality by 28%', 'RAGAS', 'MiniLM'. No generic motivational language appears in any variation.

expectations.text: The posts sound like a practitioner sharing a real learning, not a thought leader performing expertise
expectations.evidence: All three posts describe specific experimental steps, cite a failed prior assumption (expected embedding model to be the bigger lever), and reason mechanistically through the result. No prescriptive 'you should' framing, no motivational language, no performative structure. Variation 1: 'I assumed the embedding was the bottleneck — bigger model, better semantic representations, better retrieval. The data disagreed.' Variation 2: 'The chunking experiment I almost skipped — because surely the embedding model matters more — turned out to be the highest-leverage change.' These read as genuine practitioner reflections, not thought leadership performance.
